# The 'sustainability' Well-Known URI (IETF Internet-Draft)

> A uniform, out-of-band convention for web servers and digital services to publish their aggregated environmental impact, energy consumption, and carbon footprint metrics.

## Project Links
* **GitHub Repository:** https://github.com/andreibesleaga/rfc-sustainability-wellknown
* **IETF Datatracker:** https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-besleaga-sustainability-wellknown/
* **Author:** Andrei Nicolae BESLEAGA (https://github.com/andreibesleaga)
* **IETF Stream:** Independent Submission (individual; not a working-group product)
* **Current Draft Status:** `-02` is the **submitted** revision — posted to the IETF Datatracker (2026-07-03) and with the Independent Submissions Editor (ISE) for review ("Submission Received" state since 2026-07-08; frozen). `-03` is the **prepared next revision** — a breaking data-model revision (schema label `"2.0"`), to be posted when the submission window reopens after the IETF meeting.

## Context and Purpose
The digital economy consumes a significant percentage of global electricity. Regulatory frameworks (like the EU CSRD) and industry standards (like GSF SCI, W3C Web Sustainability Guidelines) increasingly require organizations to disclose their digital environmental impact. 

Initial proposals focused on per-request HTTP headers for carbon transparency, which introduced a "rebound effect" (metadata increasing the carbon footprint and reducing TTFB caching performance). This project proposes an alternative: leveraging RFC 8615 to define a discoverable `/.well-known/sustainability` URI for asynchronous, out-of-band reporting. This allows workflows where environmental impact is a primary constraint alongside cost and performance.

**Applicability is broader than conventional websites.** RFC 8615 well-known URIs are scoped to an HTTP(S) *origin* (scheme+host+port), not to "a website" — any HTTP-speaking device or service can serve one. That includes IoT/embedded devices (constrained devices already use the analogous convention, CoAP's `/.well-known/core`, registered by RFC 6690) and Web3/blockchain infrastructure (an RPC gateway or validator dashboard is an ordinary HTTP origin). The data model is entity-level, not hosting-level: `provider` names the operating entity, and the reference implementation's enterprise adapters (Salesforce NZC, Microsoft Sustainability Manager, Watershed) already populate documents from organization-level reporting platforms rather than server telemetry — so the same endpoint can carry a regulator-mandated organizational disclosure (e.g. EU CSRD, or the EU MiCA consensus-mechanism energy/carbon disclosure already required of crypto-asset issuers), not only a website's own hosting footprint.

## Repository Structure & Resources
In addition to tracking the evolution of the draft through standard IETF versions (the former `draft-besleaga-green-sustainability-wellknown-00`…`-05` series, continued as `draft-besleaga-sustainability-wellknown-00`/`-01`/`-02`, with `-03` prepared), the GitHub repository acts as an extended resource hub for implementers. It includes:
* **Draft Sources (`internet-drafts/`):** The core `.md` (Markdown) files which act as the source documents compiled into the final IETF `.xml`/`.txt` formats using the kramdown-rfc + xml2rfc toolchains, plus a `CHANGELOG.md` and a supplementary W3C Verifiable Credential structure.
* **Schemas & Validators (`schemas-validators/`):** Formal machine-readable definitions of the JSON data model — JSON Type Definition (JTD, RFC 8927) and Concise Data Definition Language (CDDL, RFC 8610) — with Python (JTD) and Ruby (CDDL) validators and a `validate-all.sh` runner.
* **Examples (`example-responses/`):** Sample JSON responses demonstrating Basic, Extended, Target-Specific, and Highly Detailed scenarios. All pass both validators.
* **Reference Implementation (`publisher/`):** A production TypeScript publisher/gateway that serves a conformant `/.well-known/sustainability` from pluggable source adapters (static/computed, Kepler/Prometheus, Climatiq, Green Web Foundation CO2.js [bytes→carbon], the Green Web Foundation carbon.txt hosted API, Salesforce Net Zero Cloud, Microsoft Sustainability Manager, Watershed). It normalizes units, validates every payload against the JTD/CDDL schemas before serving, and ships as Express/Fastify middleware and a standalone server. It can also serve a bidirectional `carbon.txt` (emit/parse/discover helper) that points back to the metrics document. Includes example security middleware (`example-scripts/`) and web-server configs (`server-configurations/`). Published on npm as **`sustainability-wellknown-publisher`** (registry 0.1.0 implements the submitted -02/"1.1" model; 0.2.0 implementing this "2.0" model is prepared, publish pending).
* **Reference Client (`consumer/`):** A TypeScript reference *client* for `/.well-known/sustainability`, complementing the publisher's reference *producer*. It fetches a document from any origin, defensively validates it (JTD schema plus the draft's cross-entry array rules), transforms it (CSV, NDJSON, a flattened one-row-per-metric shape, trend aggregation), and doubles as a standalone conformance checker (its `sustainability-fetch --strict` CLI). It ships a zero-dependency one-call `fetchSustainability` function and a richer ETag-caching `SustainabilityClient` class, and is interop-tested in-process against a real `Publisher` — demonstrating the full produce→fetch→validate→transform lifecycle across two independent implementations. Published on npm as **`sustainability-wellknown-consumer`** (same versioning note as the publisher).
* **Supporting Docs:** `ADOPTION.md` (the case for RFC/IANA adoption), `discovery/` (market scan, PRD, spec, Green Web Foundation/carbon.txt integration research), and `sfc-compliance/SFC.md` (relationship to the Sustainability-First Consensus framework).

## Key Technical Specifications

### The Endpoint
* **Path:** `/.well-known/sustainability`
* **Method:** HTTP GET (and HEAD; other methods get `405` with `Allow: GET, HEAD`)
* **Protocol:** HTTPS (SHOULD)
* **Response:** `200 OK` with `application/json` payload, or `404 Not Found` if no metadata exists. Successful responses SHOULD follow I-JSON (RFC 7493).

### Query Parameters
Servers MUST support a "Basic" service level (no parameters, aggregating the entire origin for the most recently completed published reporting period; a full calendar month is RECOMMENDED). Servers MAY support "Extended" capabilities to filter data:
* `target`: Specific resource path prefix (e.g., `?target=/api/v1`); a server scoping a response to it echoes the matched prefix in the mandatory `target` member, and SHOULD honor only a published set of prefixes.
* `period`: Timeframe as a calendar-date precision form — Year `YYYY`, Month `YYYY-MM`, or Day `YYYY-MM-DD` (only the last is an RFC 3339 `full-date`); interpreted in UTC. Without a finer `granularity`, a `period` request yields a single object (or 404 per the no-data rule), never an array.
* `granularity`: Time slices within a period (`monthly`, `daily`). When finer than the period, returns an array of objects sorted ascending (max cap of 366 objects RECOMMENDED; truncation keeps the most recent).

**Key distinction:** the *origin* is where the document is published; the `target` member is what the metrics are about — they coincide for origin-wide reports (the origin's host), but `target` may equally name an organization, a path, a cloud tenant, a device, or a data source.

### Data Model (Schema v2.0)
The response schema defines 8 mandatory + 15 optional members (23 total):
* **Mandatory Fields (8):**
  * `version`: Informational schema-revision label (e.g., "2.0").
  * `updated`: RFC 3339 timestamp of last update.
  * `capabilities`: "basic" or "extended" — describes **query-parameter support only** (a "basic" document MAY carry any optional members).
  * `provider`: Information about the publisher.
  * `measurement-method`: e.g., `hardware-metered`, `hardware-estimated`, `cloud-billing`, `third-party-modeled`.
  * `methodology-uri`: Link to the full calculation methodology (the minimum-reporting floor: a document with no numeric metric and no disclosure/attestation link MUST lead to the substantive disclosure through this URI).
  * `reporting-period`: The timeframe covered.
  * `target`: Free-form identifier of the reporting subject — origin host (RECOMMENDED for origin-wide reports), path prefix, organizational entity, cloud tenant, software source, or carbon.txt-listed site; echoes the matched prefix when the response is `target` query-scoped.
* **Optional Fields (15):** `energy-consumption` (non-negative) & `energy-unit` (Wh, kWh, MWh, GWh; default `kWh` when absent), `carbon-footprint` (gross, non-negative) & `carbon-unit` (gCO2e, kgCO2e, mtCO2e; default `gCO2e` when absent, also parameterizing scopes), `carbon-accounting` (location/market-based), `scope-1`, `scope-2`, `scope-3` emissions (MAY be negative — removals/net accounting), `sci-score` (non-negative; requires `functional-unit`), `functional-unit`, `carbon-intensity-gCO2e-per-kWh`, `estimated-annual-emissions-kgCO2e`, `renewable-energy` percentage (0–100 inclusive), `verifiable-attestation-uri`, and `disclosure-uri` (a format-agnostic URI to a machine-readable sustainability disclosure index — canonical example a Green Web Foundation carbon.txt file).
* **Omitted metrics & legacy compatibility:** omission is the only "not reported" mechanism in 2.0 — no in-band sentinel exists. Historical `1.x` documents used a negative sentinel and an optional `target-path` member; clients apply field-driven compatibility rules (a negative value in a non-negative member = not reported; a missing `target` = origin-wide report).
* **Readiness (by design):** web-ready (plain HTTPS GET, standard caching/conditional requests), API/M2M-ready (stable JSON + formal CDDL/JTD schemas + deterministic semantics), human-readable (self-describing member names + mandatory methodology link), and AI/agent-ready (fixed discoverable location, schema-validatable, no content negotiation needed).

## Important Considerations
* **Caching & DoS:** Heavy caching (e.g., `Cache-Control: max-age=86400`) and rate-limiting are strongly recommended because dynamic aggregation queries can be resource-intensive. `ETag` and `Last-Modified` headers are RECOMMENDED.
* **Privacy & Traffic Analysis:** Finer-than-24-hour granularity is discouraged to prevent correlating energy spikes with real-time user actions.
* **Hardware Fingerprinting:** An optional ~1% multiplicative (sign-preserving) "fuzzing" of metrics helps obscure specific backend hardware architectures with limited impact on aggregate accuracy; if applied, it is applied once at generation time, deterministically per reporting period, and consistently across related fields — non-negative members stay non-negative and negative scope values keep their sign.
* **Greenwashing Mitigation:** The specification does not mandate verification but provides schema surfaces (like `verifiable-attestation-uri`) to link to cryptographically signed W3C Verifiable Credentials or third-party attestations.

## Extensibility & Versioning
* The `version` member is an informational label only (no negotiation or conformance semantics); clients MUST NOT reject a document or alter processing because of its value.
* Forward compatibility rests on the must-ignore rule: implementations MUST ignore unknown fields, and the formal schemas (CDDL/JTD) are open to additional members. Extensions MAY be added under vendor namespaces (e.g., a `vendor-` prefix or domain-qualified name).
* New fields can be introduced by future specifications or privately by implementers without revising the RFC. The values `"1.0"`/`"1.1"` denote the historical pre-2.0 field set (mandatory energy/carbon members, negative not-reported sentinel, optional `target-path`); `"2.0"` denotes the current field set. Documents declaring any of these labels remain valid — clients never reject or branch on the label, and process historical documents through the field-driven compatibility rules above.
